title: The Clockmaker Who Slows Down
summary: An old clockmaker fixes the slowest clocks in the fastest city.
body:
Mr. Tanaka has a small shop in Tokyo. He fixes old clocks. His shop is on a busy street. Outside, people walk fast. They look at their phones. They check the time and run.

Inside the shop, time feels slow. There are one hundred old clocks on the walls. Some are from France. Some are from Germany. They all tick at different speeds. Tick, tock, tick. The room is full of soft sound.

Mr. Tanaka is eighty years old. He works very slowly. To fix one clock can take three weeks. He uses tiny tools. He cleans each small part by hand. "A clock is not fast," he says. "A clock is patient. It waits for the right second."

Young people often ask him a funny question. "Why fix old clocks? A phone shows the time. A phone is free and easy." Mr. Tanaka laughs. "A phone tells you the time," he says. "But an old clock teaches you to wait."

Last month, a young woman came to his shop. She was tired of her fast life. She bought a small clock for her room. Now she watches the hands move. She feels more calm. The slow clock did not save her time. It gave her something better. It gave her peace.

grammarNotes.title: Adverbs of manner
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trạng từ chỉ cách thức (như 'slowly') thường đứng sau động từ và cho biết hành động xảy ra thế nào.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Adverbs of manner tell us how something happens: 'He works very slowly.' The adverb 'slowly' comes after the verb.

grammarNotes.title: Can for ability/possibility
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng 'can' để nói về khả năng. 'Can take' nghĩa là việc đó có thể mất bao lâu.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: We use 'can' for possibility: 'To fix one clock can take three weeks.'

grammarNotes.title: Contrast with 'but'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'But' nối hai ý đối lập, làm rõ sự khác nhau giữa hai vế.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'But' shows contrast: 'A phone tells you the time, but an old clock teaches you to wait.'

translationVi:
Ông Tanaka có một cửa hàng nhỏ ở Tokyo. Ông sửa đồng hồ cũ. Cửa hàng của ông nằm trên một con phố đông đúc. Bên ngoài, người ta đi nhanh. Họ nhìn vào điện thoại. Họ xem giờ rồi chạy.

Bên trong cửa hàng, thời gian như chậm lại. Có một trăm chiếc đồng hồ cũ trên các bức tường. Một số từ Pháp. Một số từ Đức. Tất cả chạy ở những tốc độ khác nhau. Tích, tắc, tích. Căn phòng đầy âm thanh dịu nhẹ.

Ông Tanaka tám mươi tuổi. Ông làm việc rất chậm. Để sửa một chiếc đồng hồ có thể mất ba tuần. Ông dùng những dụng cụ nhỏ xíu. Ông lau từng bộ phận nhỏ bằng tay. "Đồng hồ không nhanh," ông nói. "Đồng hồ kiên nhẫn. Nó chờ đúng giây."

Người trẻ thường hỏi ông một câu buồn cười. "Sao lại sửa đồng hồ cũ? Điện thoại hiển thị giờ mà. Điện thoại miễn phí và tiện." Ông Tanaka cười. "Điện thoại cho bạn biết giờ," ông nói. "Nhưng đồng hồ cũ dạy bạn biết chờ đợi."

Tháng trước, một cô gái trẻ đến cửa hàng của ông. Cô mệt mỏi với cuộc sống vội vã. Cô mua một chiếc đồng hồ nhỏ cho phòng mình. Bây giờ cô ngắm kim đồng hồ di chuyển. Cô thấy bình tĩnh hơn. Chiếc đồng hồ chậm không tiết kiệm thời gian cho cô. Nó cho cô điều tốt hơn. Nó cho cô sự bình yên.

questions.explanation: 'Mr. Tanaka has a small shop in Tokyo.'

questions.prompt: How many old clocks are on the walls?
questions.explanation: 'There are one hundred old clocks on the walls.'

questions.prompt: How long can it take to fix one clock?
questions.explanation: 'To fix one clock can take three weeks.'

questions.prompt: How does Mr. Tanaka clean each part?
questions.explanation: 'He cleans each small part by hand.'

questions.prompt: What does Mr. Tanaka say a clock teaches you?
questions.explanation: 'An old clock teaches you to wait.'
questions.hint: What he says to the young people.

questions.prompt: Why did the young woman buy a clock?
options: She was tired of her fast life
She had no clock at all
questions.explanation: 'She was tired of her fast life.'

questions.prompt: In the passage, 'patient' is closest in meaning to:
questions.explanation: Mr. Tanaka says a clock 'is patient. It waits for the right second.'
questions.hint: Look at the sentence about waiting.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between the street and the shop?
options: The street is slow, the shop is fast
The street is fast, the shop feels slow
questions.explanation: Outside 'people walk fast,' but inside 'time feels slow.'
questions.hint: Compare paragraph one and paragraph two.

questions.prompt: What did the slow clock really give the young woman?
What is the main idea of the story?
options: Going slowly can give you peace
questions.explanation: The story shows the slow clock 'gave her peace.'
questions.hint: Think about the whole story, not one fact.

questions.prompt: Why is the young people's question called 'funny'?
options: It makes everyone laugh out loud
It misses why old clocks matter
questions.explanation: They think only about telling time, but the clock is about learning to wait.
questions.hint: Compare the question with Mr. Tanaka's answer.

blanks.explanation: He cleans each part by hand.
An old clock teaches you to wait.
She was tired of her fast life.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr. Tanaka says, 'A phone tells you the time, but an old clock teaches you to wait.' Do you agree that doing something slowly can teach us something a fast way cannot? Give one example from your own life.
